The student may have understood that the two side lengths of the rectangular bathroom floor should be multiplied to find the area. The student who selects this response understands to multiply side lengths to determine rectangular area. 6 × 7 = 42

alt.en: Question 31. Wendy cut a board into 4 pieces of equal sizes to make a table. Which fraction of the whole board does each piece represent? Choices: A: 1/4; B: 1/1; C: 4/4; D: 4/1.

text.en: “1/4”; This is the correct fraction that represents one part when a whole is partitioned into 4 equal-sized parts. The student may have understood that the denominator of the fraction represents the number of equal-sized parts the board was divided into and understood that each of the four parts represents 1/4. The student who selects this response understands a fraction 1/b is the quantity formed by 1 part when a whole is partitioned into b equal parts.
text.es: “1/4”; This is the correct fraction that represents one part when a whole is partitioned into 4 equal-sized parts. The student may have understood that the denominator of the fraction represents the number of equal-sized parts the board was divided into and understood that each of the four parts represents 1/4. The student who selects this response understands a fraction 1/b is the quantity formed by 1 part when a whole is partitioned into b equal parts.

alt.en: Question 37. What number goes in the blank to make the number sentence true? 12 × 2 = ( ? × 2) + (2 × 2) Choices: A: 10; B: 12; C: 20; D: 24.

text.en: “10”; This is the correct number that goes in the blank to make the number sentence true. The student may have understood that 12 could be represented as 10 + 2 and that (10 × 2) + (2 × 2) is equivalent to 12 × 2. The student who selects this response understands how to apply the distributive property as a strategy to multiply.
text.es: “10”; This is the correct number that goes in the blank to make the number sentence true. The student may have understood that 12 could be represented as 10 + 2 and that (10 × 2) + (2 × 2) is equivalent to 12 × 2. The student who selects this response understands how to apply the distributive property as a strategy to multiply.

alt.en: Question 38. What is 345 rounded to the nearest 100? Choices: A: 300; B: 340; C: 350; D: 400.

text.en: “300”; This is the correct result when 345 is rounded to the nearest 100. This student may have understood that 345 is situated between 300 and 400, and that the values in the tens and ones places indicate that it is closer to 300. The student who selects this response understands how to use place value to round whole numbers to the nearest 100.
text.es: “300”; This is the correct result when 345 is rounded to the nearest 100. This student may have understood that 345 is situated between 300 and 400, and that the values in the tens and ones places indicate that it is closer to 300. The student who selects this response understands how to use place value to round whole numbers to the nearest 100.

alt.en: Question 41. A number line from 0 to 1 is divided into eighths and labeled 1/8 through 7/8 at the interior tick marks. Which pair of fractions is equivalent? Choices: A: 5/8 and 2/4; B: 2/4 and 4/8; C: 3/8 and 2/4; D: 2/4 and 2/8.

text.en: “2/4 and 4/8”; This is the correct pair of equivalent fractions. The student may have understood how to divide the given number line into fourths and that 2/4 and 4/8 are the same point on the number line. The student who selects this response understands how to find an equivalent fraction.
text.es: “2/4 and 4/8”; This is the correct pair of equivalent fractions. The student may have understood how to divide the given number line into fourths and that 2/4 and 4/8 are the same point on the number line. The student who selects this response understands how to find an equivalent fraction.

alt.en: Question 43. The first row in a pattern had 5 tiles. Each row after the first had 2 more tiles than the row before it; the diagram shows rows of 5, 7, and 9 tiles. Which statement is true about the number of tiles in any row? Choices: A: It is divisible by 10; B: It is an even number; C: It is a multiple of 3; D: It is an odd number.

text.en: “It is an odd number.” This is the correct response, which describes the pattern shown. The student may have understood that the pattern was 5, 7, 9, which are all odd numbers. The student who selects this response understands how to identify or explain an arithmetic pattern.
text.es: “It is an odd number.” This is the correct response, which describes the pattern shown. The student may have understood that the pattern was 5, 7, 9, which are all odd numbers. The student who selects this response understands how to identify or explain an arithmetic pattern.

alt.en: Question 47. Which figure below has an area of 36 square units? The key says each small square equals 1 square unit. Choices: A: an irregular figure with rows containing 9, 9, 8, 8, 4, and 3 unit squares; B: an irregular figure with rows containing 6, 10, 10, and 10 unit squares; C: an irregular figure with rows containing 6, 6, 4, 2, 6, and 6 unit squares; D: an L-shaped figure with 9 squares in each of its top 2 rows and 3 squares in each of its next 4 rows.

text.en: This is the correct result when the unit squares in the figure are counted to find the area. The student may have understood that the squares in the figure could be counted to find an area of 36 square units. The student who selects this response may understand how to find the area of a figure by counting unit squares.
text.es: This is the correct result when the unit squares in the figure are counted to find the area. The student may have understood that the squares in the figure could be counted to find an area of 36 square units. The student who selects this response may understand how to find the area of a figure by counting unit squares.

alt.en: Question 1. Which model is shaded to represent a fraction equivalent to 1/2? Choices: A: a bar divided into 4 equal parts with 1 shaded; B: a bar divided into 4 equal parts with 2 shaded; C: a bar divided into 3 equal parts with 2 shaded; D: a bar divided into 6 equal parts with 2 shaded.

text.en: This is the correct representation of a fraction equivalent to 1/2. The student may have understood that an equivalent fraction can be generated by multiplying a fraction by n/n, or the student may have recognized the figure that showed 1/2 of it shaded. The student who selects this response understands how to recognize and generate equivalent fractions using a visual model.
text.es: This is the correct representation of a fraction equivalent to 1/2. The student may have understood that an equivalent fraction can be generated by multiplying a fraction by n/n, or the student may have recognized the figure that showed 1/2 of it shaded. The student who selects this response understands how to recognize and generate equivalent fractions using a visual model.

alt.en: Question 3. What is 735,286 rounded to the nearest ten thousand? Choices: A: 700,000; B: 730,000; C: 735,000; D: 740,000.

text.en: “740,000”; This is the correct response that shows 735,286 rounded to the nearest ten thousand. The student may have understood that the digit in the thousands place determines whether the number will be rounded up or down. The student who selects this response understands how to use place value understanding to round a multi-digit number to the ten thousands place.
text.es: “740,000”; This is the correct response that shows 735,286 rounded to the nearest ten thousand. The student may have understood that the digit in the thousands place determines whether the number will be rounded up or down. The student who selects this response understands how to use place value understanding to round a multi-digit number to the ten thousands place.

alt.en: Question 4. A four-sided figure has a perimeter of 37 feet. Its top side is 15 feet, its left and right sides are each 8 feet, and its bottom side has an unknown length. What is the length, in feet, of the unknown side? Choices: A: 6; B: 8; C: 14; D: 31.

text.en: “6”; This is the correct length of the missing side of the polygon. The student may have understood that the perimeter of a polygon is the sum of the lengths of all of its sides and added the given side lengths, 8 + 8 + 15 = 31, and then subtracted that from the total perimeter of the polygon, 37 – 31 = 6. The student who selects this response understands how to solve a mathematical problem to find an unknown side length of a polygon given the perimeter of the polygon.
text.es: “6”; This is the correct length of the missing side of the polygon. The student may have understood that the perimeter of a polygon is the sum of the lengths of all of its sides and added the given side lengths, 8 + 8 + 15 = 31, and then subtracted that from the total perimeter of the polygon, 37 – 31 = 6. The student who selects this response understands how to solve a mathematical problem to find an unknown side length of a polygon given the perimeter of the polygon.

alt.en: Question 5. Which figure appears to have one right angle? Choices: A: a triangle with one vertical side and one horizontal side meeting at a corner; B: a slanted parallelogram; C: a pentagon with no square corners; D: a hexagon with no square corners.

text.en: This response correctly identifies a figure that appears to have one right angle. The student may understand that a right angle equals 90º or that a right angle can be thought of visually as forming a square corner. The student who selects this response understands how to identify a right angle in a two- dimensional figure.
text.es: This response correctly identifies a figure that appears to have one right angle. The student may understand that a right angle equals 90º or that a right angle can be thought of visually as forming a square corner. The student who selects this response understands how to identify a right angle in a two- dimensional figure.

alt.en: Question 10. In the number 344,586, how many times greater is the value represented by the 4 in the ten thousands place than the value represented by the 4 in the thousands place? Choices: A: 1; B: 10; C: 1,000; D: 10,000.

text.en: “10”; This is the correct number that can be multiplied by the value of the digit 4 in the thousands place to get the value of the digit 4 in the ten-thousands place. The student may have understood that in a multi-digit whole number, a digit in one place represents ten times what it represents in the place to its right. The student who selects this response understands how to compare the value of digits given their place value.
text.es: “10”; This is the correct number that can be multiplied by the value of the digit 4 in the thousands place to get the value of the digit 4 in the ten-thousands place. The student may have understood that in a multi-digit whole number, a digit in one place represents ten times what it represents in the place to its right. The student who selects this response understands how to compare the value of digits given their place value.

alt.en: Question 11. A fraction model represents 6 whole units. Each of the 6 bars is divided into 3 equal parts, and the bottom part of each bar is shaded. Which number sentence represents the amount of the fraction model that is shaded? Choices: A: 6 × 1/2 = □; B: 6 × 1/3 = □; C: 3 × 1/6 = □; D: 3 × 1/2 = □.

text.en: “6 × 1/3 = ?”; This is the correct number sentence that represents the amount of the fraction model that is shaded. The student may have understood that each of the 6 wholes in the model is shaded to show 1/3. The student who selects this response understands a fraction a/b as a multiple of 1/b.
text.es: “6 × 1/3 = ?”; This is the correct number sentence that represents the amount of the fraction model that is shaded. The student may have understood that each of the 6 wholes in the model is shaded to show 1/3. The student who selects this response understands a fraction a/b as a multiple of 1/b.

alt.en: Question 14. A school has 17 tables in the cafeteria. Each table seats 12 students. What is the greatest number of students that can be seated at these tables? Choices: A: 114; B: 184; C: 194; D: 204.

text.en: "204"; This response shows the correct result when 17 is multiplied by 12. The student who selects this response understands how to multiply two two-digit numbers using strategies based on place value and the properties of operations.
text.es: "204"; This response shows the correct result when 17 is multiplied by 12. The student who selects this response understands how to multiply two two-digit numbers using strategies based on place value and the properties of operations.

alt.en: Question 15. Which expression represents the number 13,809 written in expanded form? Choices: A: 13 + 80 + 9; B: 13,000 + 800 + 90; C: 9 + 1,300 + 80; D: 3,000 + 10,000 + 9 + 800.

text.en: : “3,000 + 10,000 + 9 + 800”; This is the correct response that shows 13,809 written in expanded form. The student may have understood that in expanded form, each digit in the number 13,809 should be written to show its place value. The student who selects this response understands how to read and write multi-digit whole numbers using expanded form.
text.es: : “3,000 + 10,000 + 9 + 800”; This is the correct response that shows 13,809 written in expanded form. The student may have understood that in expanded form, each digit in the number 13,809 should be written to show its place value. The student who selects this response understands how to read and write multi-digit whole numbers using expanded form.

alt.en: Question 16. Three rays TS, TU, and TV share endpoint T, with ray TU between rays TS and TV. The measure of angle STV is 117°, and the measure of angle STU is 86°. The diagram is not drawn to scale. What is the measure of angle UTV? Choices: A: 31°; B: 63°; C: 157°; D: 203°.

text.en: “31°”; This is the correct measure of angle UTV. The student may have understood that angle UTV should be added to angle STU to get to the total angle measurement for angle STV of 117°. The student may also have subtracted angle STU from angle STV to find the value of angle UTV. The student who selects this response understands how to recognize angle measure as additive and solve addition and subtraction problems to find unknown angles on a diagram.
text.es: “31°”; This is the correct measure of angle UTV. The student may have understood that angle UTV should be added to angle STU to get to the total angle measurement for angle STV of 117°. The student may also have subtracted angle STU from angle STV to find the value of angle UTV. The student who selects this response understands how to recognize angle measure as additive and solve addition and subtraction problems to find unknown angles on a diagram.

alt.en: Question 17. Carter shaded two same-sized rectangular models to represent 2/3 and 8/12. The first is divided into 3 equal parts with 2 shaded; the second is divided into 12 equal parts with 8 shaded. Carter believed that 2/3 is equivalent to 8/12. Why is he correct or incorrect? Choices: A: He is incorrect because the numerator and denominator are different in 2/3 and 8/12; B: He is incorrect because the numerator and denominator in 8/12 are greater than in 2/3; C: He is correct because adding the same number to the numerator and denominator in 2/3 equals 8/12; D: He is correct because multiplying the numerator and denominator in 2/3 by the same number equals 8/12.

text.en: “He is correct because multiplying the numerator and denominator in 2/3 by the same number equals 8/12.” This is the correct response that identifies the fractions as equivalent, along with an appropriate justification. The student may have understood that an equivalent fraction can be generated by multiplying a fraction by 1 (n/n). The student who selects this response understands how to generate an equivalent fraction using the principle that fraction a/b is equivalent to fraction (n × a)/(n × b).
text.es: “He is correct because multiplying the numerator and denominator in 2/3 by the same number equals 8/12.” This is the correct response that identifies the fractions as equivalent, along with an appropriate justification. The student may have understood that an equivalent fraction can be generated by multiplying a fraction by 1 (n/n). The student who selects this response understands how to generate an equivalent fraction using the principle that fraction a/b is equivalent to fraction (n × a)/(n × b).

alt.en: Question 20. Which figure appears to have exactly one line of symmetry? Choices: A: an irregular quadrilateral with a slanted top; B: a square; C: a right-pointing pentagon shaped like a rectangle with a centered triangular point; D: a scalene triangle.

text.en: This response correctly identifies a figure with exactly one line of symmetry. The student may have recognized that there was only one way this shape could be folded to have matching sides. The student who selects this response understands how to recognize exactly one line of symmetry for a two- dimensional figure as a line across the figure where the figure can be folded along the line into matching parts.
text.es: This response correctly identifies a figure with exactly one line of symmetry. The student may have recognized that there was only one way this shape could be folded to have matching sides. The student who selects this response understands how to recognize exactly one line of symmetry for a two- dimensional figure as a line across the figure where the figure can be folded along the line into matching parts.

alt.en: Question 22. What is the measurement of the acute angle formed by a horizontal ray pointing right and a second ray rising slightly to the right? Choices: A: 15°; B: 25°; C: 165°; D: 175°.

text.en: “15°”; This is the correct response that shows the number of degrees of the angle. The student may have understood how to correctly place the protractor on the angle to get an accurate reading of the angle measure. The student who selects this response understands how to measure an angle in whole- number degrees using a protractor.
text.es: “15°”; This is the correct response that shows the number of degrees of the angle. The student may have understood how to correctly place the protractor on the angle to get an accurate reading of the angle measure. The student who selects this response understands how to measure an angle in whole- number degrees using a protractor.

alt.en: Question 25. Which figure appears to be a right triangle? Choices: A: a wide acute isosceles triangle; B: a tall acute isosceles triangle; C: a triangle with one vertical side and one horizontal side meeting at a corner; D: an obtuse scalene triangle.

text.en: This is the correct response that shows a right triangle. The student may have understood that a right triangle must have one right angle. The student who selects this response understands how to identify right triangles.
text.es: This is the correct response that shows a right triangle. The student may have understood that a right triangle must have one right angle. The student who selects this response understands how to identify right triangles.

alt.en: Question 27. A rectangular model is divided into 6 equal sections arranged in 2 rows and 3 columns. The first 2 sections in the top row are shaded. Which fraction is equivalent to the one represented by the model? Choices: A: 1/6; B: 1/3; C: 2/4; D: 2/3.

text.en: “1/3”; This is the correct fraction that is equivalent to 2/6. The student may have understood that a fraction can be multiplied by 1 (n/n) to determine an equivalent fraction. The student who selects this response may understand how to generate equivalent fractions using the fact that a/b is equivalent to a fraction (n × a)/(n × b).
text.es: “1/3”; This is the correct fraction that is equivalent to 2/6. The student may have understood that a fraction can be multiplied by 1 (n/n) to determine an equivalent fraction. The student who selects this response may understand how to generate equivalent fractions using the fact that a/b is equivalent to a fraction (n × a)/(n × b).

alt.en: Question 28. Which angle measures 25°? Choices: A: an obtuse angle formed by a horizontal right-pointing ray and a ray pointing up and left; B: an acute angle formed by a horizontal right-pointing ray and a steeper ray pointing up and right; C: an obtuse angle formed by a horizontal right-pointing ray and a shallow ray pointing up and left; D: an acute angle formed by a horizontal right-pointing ray and a shallower ray pointing up and right.

text.en: This is the correct response that shows an angle that measures 25 degrees. The student may have aligned the protractor correctly on the angle and read the measurement correctly. The student who selects this response understands how to measure angles in whole-number degrees using a protractor.
text.es: This is the correct response that shows an angle that measures 25 degrees. The student may have aligned the protractor correctly on the angle and read the measurement correctly. The student who selects this response understands how to measure angles in whole-number degrees using a protractor.

alt.en: Question 29. 4 thousands + 3 tens + 5 hundreds is less than which number below? Choices: A: 4 thousands + 5 tens + 3 hundreds; B: 8 hundreds + 3 thousands + 8 ones; C: 4 thousands + 7 ones + 8 tens + 6 hundreds; D: 9 hundreds + 9 tens + 2 thousands.

text.en: “4 thousands + 7 ones + 8 tens + 6 hundreds”; This response represents 4,687, and 4,530 < 4,687. The student may have reordered the expressions by place value to compare the numbers. The student who selects this response understands how to read multi-digit numbers in expanded form and how to compare them.
text.es: “4 thousands + 7 ones + 8 tens + 6 hundreds”; This response represents 4,687, and 4,530 < 4,687. The student may have reordered the expressions by place value to compare the numbers. The student who selects this response understands how to read multi-digit numbers in expanded form and how to compare them.

alt.en: Question 31. A circle is divided into 10 equal sections, and 4 consecutive sections on the left are shaded. Which expression represents the fraction of the figure that is shaded? Choices: A: 1/10 + 2/10 + 3/10; B: 1/10 + 1/10 + 2/10; C: 3/10 + 3/10 + 4/10; D: 4/10 + 4/10 + 4/10.

text.en: “1/10 + 1/10 + 2/10”; This is the correct response that shows that 4/10 can be decomposed into 1/10 + 1/10 + 2/10. The student may have understood that 4/10 can be decomposed into several fractions that have a total sum of 4/10. The student who selects this response understands how to decompose a fraction into a sum of fractions with the same denominator.
text.es: “1/10 + 1/10 + 2/10”; This is the correct response that shows that 4/10 can be decomposed into 1/10 + 1/10 + 2/10. The student may have understood that 4/10 can be decomposed into several fractions that have a total sum of 4/10. The student who selects this response understands how to decompose a fraction into a sum of fractions with the same denominator.

text.en: “16 4/5”; This is the correct result when 8 1/5 is added to 8 3/5. The student may have calculated 1/5 + 3/5 = 4/5 and 8 + 8 = 16. The student who selects this response understands how to add mixed numbers.
text.es: “16 4/5”; This is the correct result when 8 1/5 is added to 8 3/5. The student may have calculated 1/5 + 3/5 = 4/5 and 8 + 8 = 16. The student who selects this response understands how to add mixed numbers.

alt.en: Question 36. Which number has a 5 that represents a value ten times greater than the value represented by the 5 in 41,253? Choices: A: 31,254; B: 41,523; C: 43,125; D: 51,324.

text.en: “41,523”; This is the correct response that shows a number with a digit 5 that has a value ten times greater than the value of the digit 5 in the number 41, 253. The student may understand that the value of the digit 5 in the number 41,253 is 50, that the value of the digit 5 in the number 41,523 is 500, and that 500 is ten times as great as 50. The student who selects this response recognizes that in a multi-digit whole number, a digit in one place represents ten times the value it represents in the place to its right.
text.es: “41,523”; This is the correct response that shows a number with a digit 5 that has a value ten times greater than the value of the digit 5 in the number 41, 253. The student may understand that the value of the digit 5 in the number 41,253 is 50, that the value of the digit 5 in the number 41,523 is 500, and that 500 is ten times as great as 50. The student who selects this response recognizes that in a multi-digit whole number, a digit in one place represents ten times the value it represents in the place to its right.

alt.en: Question 42. Which fraction below can be placed in the box to make the statement □ > 3/4 true? Choices: A: 2/6; B: 5/12; C: 1/2; D: 5/6.

text.en: “5/6”; This represents the correct fraction that is greater than 3/4. The student may have found a common denominator to compare the fractions or may have compared the fractions to benchmark fractions. The student who selects this response understands how to compare two fractions with different numerators and different denominators by creating common denominators or numerators, or by comparing to a benchmark fraction. 10/12 > 9/12, or 5/6 is closer to 1 than 3/4.
text.es: “5/6”; This represents the correct fraction that is greater than 3/4. The student may have found a common denominator to compare the fractions or may have compared the fractions to benchmark fractions. The student who selects this response understands how to compare two fractions with different numerators and different denominators by creating common denominators or numerators, or by comparing to a benchmark fraction. 10/12 > 9/12, or 5/6 is closer to 1 than 3/4.

alt.en: Question 43. Which statement is represented by the equation below? 15 × 5 = 75 Choices: A: The number 15 is 5 less than 75; B: The number 15 is 5 times as many as 75; C: The number 75 is 15 more than 5; D: The number 75 is 5 times as many as 15.

text.en: “The number 75 is 5 times as many as 15.” This response represents the correct interpretation of the equation 15 × 5 = 75. The student who selects this response understands how to interpret a multiplication equation as a comparison.
text.es: “The number 75 is 5 times as many as 15.” This response represents the correct interpretation of the equation 15 × 5 = 75. The student who selects this response understands how to interpret a multiplication equation as a comparison.

alt.en: Question 44. Alex bought 2 printer cartridges for $28 each and a printer for $85. He gave the cashier $150. How much change should Alex have received from the cashier? Choices: A: $9; B: $11; C: $19; D: $37.

text.en: "$9"; This response represents the correct amount of change that should be received. This can be calculated by finding the total price of the 2 printer cartridges and the printer, then subtracting that amount from $150. The student who selects this response understands how to use the four operations to solve a multistep word problem posed with whole numbers that has a whole-number answer. 2 × 28 + 85 = 141 150 – 141 = 9
text.es: "$9"; This response represents the correct amount of change that should be received. This can be calculated by finding the total price of the 2 printer cartridges and the printer, then subtracting that amount from $150. The student who selects this response understands how to use the four operations to solve a multistep word problem posed with whole numbers that has a whole-number answer. 2 × 28 + 85 = 141 150 – 141 = 9

alt.en: Question 48. A diagram shows a book standing vertically on a horizontal shelf, forming a square corner. What is the measure of the angle formed by the book and the shelf? Choices: A: 45 degrees; B: 90 degrees; C: 180 degrees; D: 360 degrees.

text.en: “90 degrees”; This is the correct number of degrees in the angle formed by the book and the shelf. The student may have recognized that the angle was formed by perpendicular lines or measured the angle with a protractor. The student who selects this response understands how to recognize an angle measure of n degrees.
text.es: “90 degrees”; This is the correct number of degrees in the angle formed by the book and the shelf. The student may have recognized that the angle was formed by perpendicular lines or measured the angle with a protractor. The student who selects this response understands how to recognize an angle measure of n degrees.

alt.en: Question 49. A table titled Bags of Trail Mix shows: mass 2 1/2 pounds, 1 bag; mass 1 1/2 pounds, 3 bags; mass 1/2 pound, 4 bags. Which line plot represents the data in the table? Choices: A: a line plot from 0 to 3 with half-pound tick marks, showing 1 X at 1/2, 3 Xs at 1, and 4 Xs at 1 1/2; B: a line plot from 0 to 4 with whole-number tick marks, showing 1 X halfway between 0 and 1, 1 X halfway between 1 and 2, and 1 X halfway between 2 and 3; C: a line plot from 0 to 3 with half-pound tick marks, showing 4 Xs at 1/2, 3 Xs at 1 1/2, and 1 X at 2 1/2; D: a line plot from 0 to 3 with half-pound tick marks, showing 1 X each at 1/2, 1 1/2, and 2 1/2.

text.en: This is the correct line plot for the data. The student may have understood that each X on the line plot stood for one bag of trail mix, so if the table shows 4 bags have a mass of 1/2 pound, then there should be 4 Xs above the 1/2 tick mark on the line plot. If the table shows 3 bags have a mass of 1 1/2 pounds, then there should be 3 Xs above the 1 1/2 tick mark on the line plot. If the table shows 1 bag has a mass of 2 1/2 pounds, then there should be 1 X above the 2 1/2 tick mark on the line plot. The student who selects this response understands how to recognize a line plot that displays a data set of measurements in fractions of a unit.
text.es: This is the correct line plot for the data. The student may have understood that each X on the line plot stood for one bag of trail mix, so if the table shows 4 bags have a mass of 1/2 pound, then there should be 4 Xs above the 1/2 tick mark on the line plot. If the table shows 3 bags have a mass of 1 1/2 pounds, then there should be 3 Xs above the 1 1/2 tick mark on the line plot. If the table shows 1 bag has a mass of 2 1/2 pounds, then there should be 1 X above the 2 1/2 tick mark on the line plot. The student who selects this response understands how to recognize a line plot that displays a data set of measurements in fractions of a unit.

alt.en: Question 1. Which statement is true about the values of the two expressions below? Expression A: 3 × (8 + 4). Expression B: 8 + 4. Choices: A: The value of Expression B is three times the value of Expression A. B: The value of Expression A is three times the value of Expression B. C: The value of Expression A is three more than the value of Expression B. D: The value of Expression B is three more than the value of Expression A.

text.en: “The value of Expression A is three times the value of Expression B.” This is the correct interpretation of Expression A and Expression B. The student may have understood that multiplying the expression 8 + 4 by 3 would make it 3 times as great as the original expression. The student who selects this response understands how to interpret numerical expressions without evaluating them.
text.es: “The value of Expression A is three times the value of Expression B.” This is the correct interpretation of Expression A and Expression B. The student may have understood that multiplying the expression 8 + 4 by 3 would make it 3 times as great as the original expression. The student who selects this response understands how to interpret numerical expressions without evaluating them.

alt.en: Question 2. Which diagram represents a volume of one cubic unit? Choices: A: A two-dimensional square measuring 1 unit high and 1 unit wide. B: A two-dimensional square measuring 2 units high and 2 units wide. C: A three-dimensional rectangular prism measuring 2 units high, 2 units wide, and 2 units deep; dashed lines indicate the hidden edges. D: A three-dimensional cube measuring 1 unit high, 1 unit wide, and 1 unit deep; dashed lines indicate the hidden edges.

text.en: This is the correct response that shows a diagram that represents 1 cubic unit. The student who selects this response understands that a cube with side length 1 unit, called a “unit cube,” is said to have “one cubic unit” of volume.
text.es: This is the correct response that shows a diagram that represents 1 cubic unit. The student who selects this response understands that a cube with side length 1 unit, called a “unit cube,” is said to have “one cubic unit” of volume.

alt.en: Question 5. Which phrase describes the volume of a 3-dimensional figure? Choices: A: the number of square units it takes to fill a solid figure. B: the number of cubic units it takes to fill a solid figure. C: the number of square units it takes to cover the outside of a solid figure. D: the number of cubic units it takes to cover the outside of a solid figure.

text.en: “the number of cubic units it takes to fill a solid figure”; This is the correct description of the volume of a solid figure as the number of cubic units that it takes to fill the figure. A student who selects this response understands that a solid figure, which can be packed without gaps or overlaps using n unit cubes, is said to have a volume of n cubic units.
text.es: “the number of cubic units it takes to fill a solid figure”; This is the correct description of the volume of a solid figure as the number of cubic units that it takes to fill the figure. A student who selects this response understands that a solid figure, which can be packed without gaps or overlaps using n unit cubes, is said to have a volume of n cubic units.

alt.en: Question 7. A recipe for 1 batch of muffins included 2/3 cup of raisins. Ina made 2 1/2 batches of muffins. How many cups of raisins did she use? Choices: A: 1 4/6. B: 1 5/6. C: 2 2/6. D: 3 1/6.

text.en: “1 4/6 cups” The student correctly multiplied 2/3 by 2 1/2. Converting the mixed number gives (2/3) × (5/2) = 10/6 = 1 4/6 cups.
text.es: “1 4/6 cups” The student correctly multiplied 2/3 by 2 1/2. Converting the mixed number gives (2/3) × (5/2) = 10/6 = 1 4/6 cups.

alt.en: Question 9. Mani, James, and Isidro equally shared 1/2 of a pie. Which fraction of the whole pie did each of them receive? Choices: A: 1/6. B: 1/5. C: 2/3. D: 3/2.

text.en: “1/6” This is the fraction of the whole pie each person received. Dividing 1/2 by 3 is the same as multiplying by 1/3: (1/2) × (1/3) = 1/6.
text.es: “1/6” This is the fraction of the whole pie each person received. Dividing 1/2 by 3 is the same as multiplying by 1/3: (1/2) × (1/3) = 1/6.

alt.en: Question 10. A number is given below: 136.25. In a different number, the 6 represents a value which is one-tenth of the value of the 6 in the number above. What value is represented by the 6 in the other number? Choices: A: six hundredths. B: six tenths. C: six ones. D: six tens.

text.en: “six tenths” This is the correct value represented by the 6 in the other number. In a multi-digit number, a digit in one place represents 10 times as much as it represents in the place to its right and 1/10 of what it represents in the place to its left.
text.es: “six tenths” This is the correct value represented by the 6 in the other number. In a multi-digit number, a digit in one place represents 10 times as much as it represents in the place to its right and 1/10 of what it represents in the place to its left.

alt.en: Question 13. Austin collected 30 9/10 kilograms of glass for recycling. Exactly 2/3 of the glass he collected was blue. What was the total amount, in kilograms, of blue glass Austin collected? Choices: A: 20 3/5. B: 27 2/3. C: 30 3/5. D: 30 11/13.

text.en: “20 3/5 kilograms” The student correctly converted 30 9/10 to 309/10 and multiplied by 2/3: (309/10) × (2/3) = 618/30 = 20 18/30 = 20 3/5.
text.es: “20 3/5 kilograms” The student correctly converted 30 9/10 to 309/10 and multiplied by 2/3: (309/10) × (2/3) = 618/30 = 20 18/30 = 20 3/5.

alt.en: Question 14. What number goes in the blank to make the statement below true? 3,840 ounces = _____ pounds. Choices: A: 24. B: 240. C: 480. D: 61,440.

text.en: “240”; This response represents a correct conversion from ounces to pounds. Using the relationship “1 pound = 16 ounces,” the student may have divided 3,840 ounces by 16 obtains a result of 240 pounds. A student who selects this response understands how to correctly convert among different-sized standard measurement units.
text.es: “240”; This response represents a correct conversion from ounces to pounds. Using the relationship “1 pound = 16 ounces,” the student may have divided 3,840 ounces by 16 obtains a result of 240 pounds. A student who selects this response understands how to correctly convert among different-sized standard measurement units.

alt.en: Question 16. What is the area, in square inches, of a rectangle with the dimensions shown in the diagram below? The rectangle measures 7/8 inch in length and 3/16 inch in width. Choices: A: 21/128. B: 3/14. C: 10/24. D: 24/112.

text.en: “21/128 square inch” This is the correct area of the rectangle. Multiplying the fractional side lengths gives (7/8) × (3/16) = 21/128.
text.es: “21/128 square inch” This is the correct area of the rectangle. Multiplying the fractional side lengths gives (7/8) × (3/16) = 21/128.

alt.en: Question 18. What is the value of the expression below? 1,536 ÷ 24. Choices: A: 57. B: 64. C: 65. D: 68.

text.en: “64”; This response represents the correct value of the expression. The student may have correctly performed the traditional algorithm for division with precision. Alternatively, the student may have used estimation to rule out options A and D and then used an understanding of the relationship between multiplication and division to determine whether 64 or 65 was the quotient.
text.es: “64”; This response represents the correct value of the expression. The student may have correctly performed the traditional algorithm for division with precision. Alternatively, the student may have used estimation to rule out options A and D and then used an understanding of the relationship between multiplication and division to determine whether 64 or 65 was the quotient.

alt.en: Question 19. What is the volume, in cubic centimeters, of the figure below? The figure is a rectangular prism made of equal unit cubes. Its front face is 5 cubes wide and 3 cubes high, and it is 3 cubes deep. A separate key shows one cube labeled “1 cubic centimeter.” Choices: A: 15. B: 24. C: 30. D: 45.

text.en: “45”; This response represents the correct volume of the figure. The student may have counted the number of unit cubes in one layer of the rectangular prism and then used addition or multiplication to determine the total number of unit cubes as the volume, in cubic centimeters, of the figure. Alternatively, the student may have applied the formula for volume of a rectangular prism, using the length, width, and height values determined by counting the number of unit cubes along each dimension of the figure. 5 × 3 × 3 = 45 A student who selects this response understands how to measure the volume of a figure by counting unit cubes.
text.es: “45”; This response represents the correct volume of the figure. The student may have counted the number of unit cubes in one layer of the rectangular prism and then used addition or multiplication to determine the total number of unit cubes as the volume, in cubic centimeters, of the figure. Alternatively, the student may have applied the formula for volume of a rectangular prism, using the length, width, and height values determined by counting the number of unit cubes along each dimension of the figure. 5 × 3 × 3 = 45 A student who selects this response understands how to measure the volume of a figure by counting unit cubes.

alt.en: Question 23. Millie designed a rectangular label to put on the front of her scrapbook. The label was 5/12 foot wide and 5/6 foot long. What was the area, in square feet, of the label? Choices: A: 2 6/12. B: 1 3/12. C: 10/18. D: 25/72.

text.en: “25/72 square foot” This is the correct area of the rectangular label. Multiplying its fractional side lengths gives (5/12) × (5/6) = 25/72.
text.es: “25/72 square foot” This is the correct area of the rectangular label. Multiplying its fractional side lengths gives (5/12) × (5/6) = 25/72.

alt.en: Question 27. Which expression means the same as the phrase below? Subtract 3 from the product of 8 and 5. Choices: A: (5 × 8) + 3. B: (5 × 8) − 3. C: 5 × (8 − 3). D: 5 × (8 + 3).

text.en: “ (5 × 8) – 3”; This response correctly represents the given phrase as a numerical expression. The given phrase describes a calculation in which the product of 8 and 5 is found first and 3 is then subtracted from the product. A student who selects this response understands how to write a simple expression that records calculations with numbers.
text.es: “ (5 × 8) – 3”; This response correctly represents the given phrase as a numerical expression. The given phrase describes a calculation in which the product of 8 and 5 is found first and 3 is then subtracted from the product. A student who selects this response understands how to write a simple expression that records calculations with numbers.

alt.en: Question 32. Jim gave the following description of a figure: It is a quadrilateral. All sides are equal in length. There are two equal obtuse angles and two equal acute angles. Which figure could match Jim’s description? Choices: A: rectangle. B: rhombus. C: square. D: pentagon.

text.en: “rhombus”; This is the correct figure that could match Jim’s description. The student may have known that a rhombus is a quadrilateral with sides of equal length and could have two equal obtuse and two acute equal angles. The student who selects this response understands how to classify two- dimensional figures in a hierarchy based on properties.
text.es: “rhombus”; This is the correct figure that could match Jim’s description. The student may have known that a rhombus is a quadrilateral with sides of equal length and could have two equal obtuse and two acute equal angles. The student who selects this response understands how to classify two- dimensional figures in a hierarchy based on properties.

alt.en: Question 33. Which expression is equivalent to 100,000? Choices: A: 10⁴. B: 10⁵. C: 10⁶. D: 10⁷.

text.en: “10⁵” This expression is equivalent to 100,000 because the exponent 5 means multiplying five factors of 10: 10 × 10 × 10 × 10 × 10 = 100,000.
text.es: “10⁵” This expression is equivalent to 100,000 because the exponent 5 means multiplying five factors of 10: 10 × 10 × 10 × 10 × 10 = 100,000.

alt.en: Question 34. Lincoln had 2 books in his backpack. One book had a mass of 3 pounds 7 ounces, and the other book had a mass of 2 pounds 10 ounces. What was the total mass, in ounces, of the books? Choices: A: 60. B: 77. C: 80. D: 97.

text.en: “97”; This is the correct response when the masses of both books are added. The student may have understood that there are 16 ounces in a pound, converted 3 pounds 7 ounces and 2 pounds 10 ounces to ounces, 3 × 16 = 48, 48 + 7 = 55 and 2 × 16 = 32, 32 + 10 = 42, and then added the two masses, 55 + 42 = 97. The student who selects this response understands how to convert among pounds and ounces and use these conversions to solve a multi-step, real-world problem.
text.es: “97”; This is the correct response when the masses of both books are added. The student may have understood that there are 16 ounces in a pound, converted 3 pounds 7 ounces and 2 pounds 10 ounces to ounces, 3 × 16 = 48, 48 + 7 = 55 and 2 × 16 = 32, 32 + 10 = 42, and then added the two masses, 55 + 42 = 97. The student who selects this response understands how to convert among pounds and ounces and use these conversions to solve a multi-step, real-world problem.

alt.en: Question 36. A box contains 512 grams of cereal. One serving of cereal is 56 grams. How many servings of cereal does the box contain? Choices: A: 9 1/4. B: 9 1/8. C: 9 8/56. D: 9 8/512.

text.en: “9 8/56 servings” The student correctly divided 512 by 56. The quotient is 9 with remainder 8, which can be written as the mixed number 9 8/56.
text.es: “9 8/56 servings” The student correctly divided 512 by 56. The quotient is 9 with remainder 8, which can be written as the mixed number 9 8/56.

alt.en: Question 38. Lori and Maria bought juice to make fruit punch. Maria bought 5 bottles of juice, each containing 750 milliliters. Lori bought 4 liters of juice. Based on this information, which sentence is true? Choices: A: Lori bought 0.25 liter more juice than Maria. B: Maria bought 0.75 liter more juice than Lori. C: Maria bought 33.5 liters more juice than Lori. D: Lori bought 36.25 liters more juice than Maria.

text.en: “Lori bought 0.25 liter more juice than Maria.” This is the correct response. The student may have multiplied 750 milliliters by 5 to get 3,750 milliliters for Maria and converted those milliliters to 3.75 liters because there are 1,000 milliliters in a liter. The student may then have subtracted 3.75 from 4 to get 0.25. The student who selects this response understands how to convert among liters and milliliters and use these conversions to solve a multi-step, real-world problem.
text.es: “Lori bought 0.25 liter more juice than Maria.” This is the correct response. The student may have multiplied 750 milliliters by 5 to get 3,750 milliliters for Maria and converted those milliliters to 3.75 liters because there are 1,000 milliliters in a liter. The student may then have subtracted 3.75 from 4 to get 0.25. The student who selects this response understands how to convert among liters and milliliters and use these conversions to solve a multi-step, real-world problem.

alt.en: Question 39. Which decimal best represents the location of point X on the number line? The horizontal number line has arrows pointing in both directions and 11 equally spaced tick marks. The first tick is labeled 50/100, and the last tick is labeled 60/100. Point X is marked by a closed dot on the sixth tick from the left, five equal intervals to the right of 50/100. Choices: A: 0.5. B: 0.55. C: 0.56. D: 0.6.

text.en: “0.55”; This is the correct decimal that names the location of point X. The student may have understood that each interval on the number line is equal to 1 hundredth and then counted the intervals from 50 to 55 hundredths. The student who selects this response understands how to use decimal notation for fractions with denominators of 100 on a number line.
text.es: “0.55”; This is the correct decimal that names the location of point X. The student may have understood that each interval on the number line is equal to 1 hundredth and then counted the intervals from 50 to 55 hundredths. The student who selects this response understands how to use decimal notation for fractions with denominators of 100 on a number line.

alt.en: Question 40. Clark made a model of his house. His house is 30 1/2 feet long. The dimensions of the model were 1/25 the dimensions of Clark’s actual home. What is the length, in feet, of the model? Choices: A: 1 10/50. B: 1 11/50. C: 30 23/50. D: 30 27/50.

text.en: “1 11/50 feet” The student correctly converted 30 1/2 to 61/2 and applied the 1/25 scale: (61/2) × (1/25) = 61/50 = 1 11/50 feet.
text.es: “1 11/50 feet” The student correctly converted 30 1/2 to 61/2 and applied the 1/25 scale: (61/2) × (1/25) = 61/50 = 1 11/50 feet.

alt.en: Question 41. Deb has a board that measures 5 feet in length. How many 1/4-foot-long pieces can Deb cut from the board? Choices: A: 1. B: 9. C: 10. D: 20.

text.en: “20” This is the number of 1/4-foot pieces that Deb can cut from a 5-foot board. Dividing by 1/4 is the same as multiplying by 4: 5 ÷ (1/4) = 5 × 4 = 20.
text.es: “20” This is the number of 1/4-foot pieces that Deb can cut from a 5-foot board. Dividing by 1/4 is the same as multiplying by 4: 5 ÷ (1/4) = 5 × 4 = 20.

alt.en: Question 43. In which number does the 5 represent a value 10 times the value represented by the 5 in 35,187? Choices: A: 117,568. B: 247,351. C: 325,827. D: 453,362.

text.en: “453,362” The digit 5 represents 50,000, which is 10 times the 5,000 represented by the digit 5 in 35,187. In a multi-digit number, each place is worth 10 times the place to its right and 1/10 of the place to its left.
text.es: “453,362” The digit 5 represents 50,000, which is 10 times the 5,000 represented by the digit 5 in 35,187. In a multi-digit number, each place is worth 10 times the place to its right and 1/10 of the place to its left.

alt.en: Question 44. Michele is 52 inches tall. Her father is 6 feet 3 inches tall. Exactly how many inches taller is Michele’s father than Michele? Choices: A: 11. B: 13. C: 23. D: 25.

text.en: “23” There are 12 inches in 1 foot, so 6 feet 3 inches equals (6 × 12) + 3 = 75 inches. Michele's father is therefore 75 - 52 = 23 inches taller than Michele.
text.es: “23” There are 12 inches in 1 foot, so 6 feet 3 inches equals (6 × 12) + 3 = 75 inches. Michele's father is therefore 75 - 52 = 23 inches taller than Michele.

alt.en: Question 49. What is the value of 2/5 + 3/7? Choices: A: 6/35. B: 5/12. C: 6/12. D: 29/35.

text.en: “29/35” This is the correct sum of 2/5 and 3/7. Using a common denominator of 35 gives 2/5 = 14/35 and 3/7 = 15/35, so 14/35 + 15/35 = 29/35.
text.es: “29/35” This is the correct sum of 2/5 and 3/7. Using a common denominator of 35 gives 2/5 = 14/35 and 3/7 = 15/35, so 14/35 + 15/35 = 29/35.

title.en: New York Grade 6 Math — 2014 Released Questions
title.es: Matemáticas de Nueva York, grado 6 — preguntas publicadas de 2014

description.en: Practice the multiple-choice questions officially released from the 2014 New York State Grade 6 Mathematics Test.
description.es: Practica las preguntas de opción múltiple publicadas oficialmente del examen de Matemáticas de 6.º grado del Estado de Nueva York de 2014.

sourceTitle.en: 2014 NYS Grade 6 Mathematics Test Released Questions
sourceTitle.es: Preguntas publicadas del examen de Matemáticas de 6.º grado del Estado de Nueva York de 2014

alt.en: Question 1. What is the value of the expression below? 3⁴ + 9. Choices: A: 21. B: 39. C: 43. D: 90.

text.en: “90” This is the correct value of the numerical expression. Evaluating the exponent first gives 3⁴ + 9 = (3 × 3 × 3 × 3) + 9 = 81 + 9 = 90.
text.es: “90” This is the correct value of the numerical expression. Evaluating the exponent first gives 3⁴ + 9 = (3 × 3 × 3 × 3) + 9 = 81 + 9 = 90.

alt.en: Question 2. Paul bought a package of 6 spiral notebooks for a total cost of $13.50. Which equation represents p, the cost, in dollars, of each notebook? Choices: A: p = 13.50 − 6. B: p = 13.50 × 6. C: p = 13.50 + 6. D: p = 13.50 ÷ 6.

text.en: “p = 13.50 ÷ 6”; This response shows the correct equation that represents the situation; the total cost, $13.50, divided by the number of notebooks, 6, will yield the cost of each notebook, p. A student who selects this response understands how to solve real-world problems by writing equations of the form px = q for cases in which p,q, and x are all nonnegative rational numbers.
text.es: “p = 13.50 ÷ 6”; This response shows the correct equation that represents the situation; the total cost, $13.50, divided by the number of notebooks, 6, will yield the cost of each notebook, p. A student who selects this response understands how to solve real-world problems by writing equations of the form px = q for cases in which p,q, and x are all nonnegative rational numbers.

alt.en: Question 5. The set of numbers 1, 7, 11, and 36 contains values for m. What value of m makes the equation below true? 4m + 8 = 36. Choices: A: 1. B: 7. C: 11. D: 36.

text.en: “7”; This answer represents the value of m that makes the given equation true. The student may have used a method such as the one below. 4(7)+ 8 = 36 28 + 8 = 36 36 = 36 A student who selects this response understands how to use substitution to determine whether a given number in a specified set makes an equation or inequality true.
text.es: “7”; This answer represents the value of m that makes the given equation true. The student may have used a method such as the one below. 4(7)+ 8 = 36 28 + 8 = 36 36 = 36 A student who selects this response understands how to use substitution to determine whether a given number in a specified set makes an equation or inequality true.

alt.en: Question 10. What is the value of the expression below when c = 5 and d = 4? 6c² − 5d + 8. Choices: A: 48. B: 79. C: 138. D: 888.

text.en: “138” This is the correct value of the expression when c = 5 and d = 4. Substitution and the order of operations give 6(5²) - 5(4) + 8 = 150 - 20 + 8 = 138.
text.es: “138” This is the correct value of the expression when c = 5 and d = 4. Substitution and the order of operations give 6(5²) - 5(4) + 8 = 150 - 20 + 8 = 138.

alt.en: Question 11. Which expression is equivalent to 16a + 24b? Choices: A: 4(4a + 20b). B: 8(2a + 3b). C: 4a(4 + 6b). D: 8ab(2 + 3).

text.en: “8(2a + 3b)”; This answer represents an expression that is equivalent to the given expression. The student may have used a method such as the one below. 16a + 24b = 8(2a)+ 8(3b) = 8(2a + 3b) A student who selects this response understands how to apply the distributive property to generate equivalent expressions.
text.es: “8(2a + 3b)”; This answer represents an expression that is equivalent to the given expression. The student may have used a method such as the one below. 16a + 24b = 8(2a)+ 8(3b) = 8(2a + 3b) A student who selects this response understands how to apply the distributive property to generate equivalent expressions.

alt.en: Question 12. Felicity babysat 2 hours each night for 10 nights. She earned a total of $180 babysitting. Felicity wants to calculate her hourly rate. How much did Felicity earn per hour babysitting? Choices: A: $9. B: $15. C: $18. D: $20.

text.en: “$9”; This answer represents the amount Felicity earned per hour babysitting. The student may have used a method such as the one below. Total number of hours Felicity babysat: 2×10 = 20 Felicity’s hourly rate: 180 ÷ 20 = 9 A student who selects this response understands the concept of a unit rate presented in a context that uses rate language.
text.es: “$9”; This answer represents the amount Felicity earned per hour babysitting. The student may have used a method such as the one below. Total number of hours Felicity babysat: 2×10 = 20 Felicity’s hourly rate: 180 ÷ 20 = 9 A student who selects this response understands the concept of a unit rate presented in a context that uses rate language.

alt.en: Question 18. Which expression is equivalent to 3(6m) + m? Choices: A: 19m. B: 21m. C: 7m + 3. D: 18m + 6m².

text.en: “19m”; This response correctly shows an expression that is equivalent to the given expression. The student may have used a method such as the one below. 3(6m)+ m = 18m + m = 19m A student who selects this response understands how to apply the properties of operations to generate equivalent expressions.
text.es: “19m”; This response correctly shows an expression that is equivalent to the given expression. The student may have used a method such as the one below. 3(6m)+ m = 18m + m = 19m A student who selects this response understands how to apply the properties of operations to generate equivalent expressions.

alt.en: Question 20. Machines S and T were both cleaned this week. Machine S is cleaned every 12 weeks. Machine T is cleaned every 8 weeks. What is the fewest number of weeks that will pass before both machines are cleaned again in the same week? Choices: A: 16. B: 24. C: 36. D: 48.

text.en: “24”; This response correctly shows the fewest number of weeks that will pass before both machines are cleaned again. The student may have used a method such as the one below. Machine S: 12, 24, 36, ... Machine T: 8, 16, 24, 32, ... 12×2 = 24 Least number of weeks/least common multiple: 8×3 = 24 A student who selects this response understands how to find the least common multiple of two whole numbers less than or equal to 12.
text.es: “24”; This response correctly shows the fewest number of weeks that will pass before both machines are cleaned again. The student may have used a method such as the one below. Machine S: 12, 24, 36, ... Machine T: 8, 16, 24, 32, ... 12×2 = 24 Least number of weeks/least common multiple: 8×3 = 24 A student who selects this response understands how to find the least common multiple of two whole numbers less than or equal to 12.

alt.en: Question 23. Nadia bought 5 tickets to attend a spaghetti supper fundraiser at her school. The equation 5x = 32.50 can be used to find x, the cost of each ticket in dollars. Which equation represents the cost of each ticket? Choices: A: x = 32.50/5. B: x = 32.50(5). C: x = 32.50 − 5. D: x = 32.50 + 5.

text.en: “x = 32.50/5” This equation correctly shows the cost of each ticket. Dividing both sides of 5x = 32.50 by 5 gives x = 32.50/5 = 6.50.
text.es: “x = 32.50/5” This equation correctly shows the cost of each ticket. Dividing both sides of 5x = 32.50 by 5 gives x = 32.50/5 = 6.50.

alt.en: Question 25. The area of a rectangular city park is 25/54 square miles. The length of the park is 5/9 mile. What is the width, in miles, of the park? Choices: A: 4/9. B: 5/6. C: 1 1/54. D: 1 1/5.

text.en: “5/6 mile” This is the correct width of the park. Using A = lw and dividing the area by the length gives (25/54) ÷ (5/9) = (25/54) × (9/5) = 5/6 mile.
text.es: “5/6 mile” This is the correct width of the park. Using A = lw and dividing the area by the length gives (25/54) ÷ (5/9) = (25/54) × (9/5) = 5/6 mile.

alt.en: Question 26. The table below shows how much money a grocery store receives for selling different amounts of asparagus. A table titled “ASPARAGUS SALES” has columns “Number of Pounds” and “Total Sales.” Its rows are: 4 pounds, $10; 6 pounds, $15; 8 pounds, $20; 10 pounds, unknown; 12 pounds, unknown. If the unit rate is constant, what are the total sales for 12 pounds of asparagus? Choices: A: $22.50. B: $25.00. C: $30.00. D: $32.50.

text.en: “$30.00” The ratios give the same unit rate: $10/4 lb = $15/6 lb = $20/8 lb = $5/2 lb. Applying that rate to 12 pounds gives 12 lb × ($5/2 lb) = $30.
text.es: “$30.00” The ratios give the same unit rate: $10/4 lb = $15/6 lb = $20/8 lb = $5/2 lb. Applying that rate to 12 pounds gives 12 lb × ($5/2 lb) = $30.

alt.en: Question 27. Point Q is plotted on a coordinate grid at (−3, −2), three units left of the y-axis and two units below the x-axis. The x- and y-axes intersect at the origin, each grid interval represents 1 unit, and the labeled values on each axis run from −5 through 5. Which statement correctly describes the relationship between the point (−3, 2) and point Q? Choices: A: It is a reflection across the x-axis. B: It is a reflection across the y-axis. C: They are 6 units apart. D: They are 2 units apart.

text.en: “It is a reflection across the x-axis.” Point Q is (−3, −2), while the other point is (−3, 2). Their x-coordinates are the same and their y-coordinates are opposites, so the points are reflections of each other across the x-axis.
text.es: “It is a reflection across the x-axis.” Point Q is (−3, −2), while the other point is (−3, 2). Their x-coordinates are the same and their y-coordinates are opposites, so the points are reflections of each other across the x-axis.

alt.en: Question 28. What is the value of the expression below when z = 7? The expression is 3z − 3. Choices: A: 12. B: 18. C: 21. D: 34.

text.en: “18”; This response represents the correct value of the given expression when z = 7. The student may have used a method such as the one below. 3(7)−3 = 18 A student who selects this response understands how to evaluate expressions at specific values of their variables, using the correct order of operations.
text.es: “18”; This response represents the correct value of the given expression when z = 7. The student may have used a method such as the one below. 3(7)−3 = 18 A student who selects this response understands how to evaluate expressions at specific values of their variables, using the correct order of operations.

alt.en: Question 29. Which equation is true when n = 4? Choices: A: 2n = 6. B: n + 3 = 7. C: 9 − n = 13. D: n/12 = 3.

text.en: “n + 3 = 7”; This response represents the correct equation that is true when n = 4; when replacing the variable n with 4, the result is a true statement, 4 + 3 = 7. A student who selects this response understands how to use substitution to determine whether a given number makes an equation true. 4 + 3 = 7
text.es: “n + 3 = 7”; This response represents the correct equation that is true when n = 4; when replacing the variable n with 4, the result is a true statement, 4 + 3 = 7. A student who selects this response understands how to use substitution to determine whether a given number makes an equation true. 4 + 3 = 7

alt.en: Question 30. What coordinates best represent the location of point K on the coordinate plane below? The x- and y-axes intersect at the origin. Both axes are labeled at intervals of 2 from −10 through 10, and the grid lines are spaced every 2 units. Point K lies in Quadrant III at x = −7, halfway between x = −8 and x = −6, and at y = −4. Choices: A: (−7, −4). B: (−7, 4). C: (−4, −7). D: (−4, 7).

text.en: “(−7,−4)”; This response correctly identifies the x- and y-coordinates for the given point on the coordinate plane. A student who selects this response understands how to find and position a pair of integers on a coordinate plane.
text.es: “(−7,−4)”; This response correctly identifies the x- and y-coordinates for the given point on the coordinate plane. A student who selects this response understands how to find and position a pair of integers on a coordinate plane.

alt.en: Question 31. In Ms. Perron’s class, 75% of the students are boys. There are 18 boys in the class. What is the total number of students in Ms. Perron’s class? Choices: A: 6. B: 14. C: 24. D: 57.

text.en: “24” There are 24 students in the class. If n is the total number of students, then 18/n = 75/100 because 18 boys are 75% of the class. Solving the proportion gives n = 24.
text.es: “24” There are 24 students in the class. If n is the total number of students, then 18/n = 75/100 because 18 boys are 75% of the class. Solving the proportion gives n = 24.

alt.en: Question 36. Which expression is equivalent to 5(d + 1)? Choices: A: 5d + 5. B: 5d + 1. C: d + 5. D: d + 6.

text.en: “5d + 5”; This answer represents an expression that is equivalent to the given expression. The student may have made this determination using the distributive property, as shown below: 5(d +1) = 5d + 5 A student who selects this response understands how to apply the distributive property to generate equivalent expressions.
text.es: “5d + 5”; This answer represents an expression that is equivalent to the given expression. The student may have made this determination using the distributive property, as shown below: 5(d +1) = 5d + 5 A student who selects this response understands how to apply the distributive property to generate equivalent expressions.

alt.en: Question 37. A net of a square pyramid consists of a central square with one triangular face attached to each of its four sides. Each side of the square is 5.1 cm. The perpendicular height of each triangular face, from the midpoint of its base to its opposite vertex, is 5.95 cm; a right-angle marker indicates that this height is perpendicular to the base. What is the surface area, in square centimeters, of the pyramid? Choices: A: 60.7. B: 86.7. C: 121.4. D: 147.4.

text.en: “86.7” Each triangular face has area (1/2) × 5.95 × 5.1 = 15.1725 square centimeters, and the square base has area 5.1 × 5.1 = 26.01. Thus the total surface area is 4(15.1725) + 26.01 = 86.7 square centimeters.
text.es: “86.7” Each triangular face has area (1/2) × 5.95 × 5.1 = 15.1725 square centimeters, and the square base has area 5.1 × 5.1 = 26.01. Thus the total surface area is 4(15.1725) + 26.01 = 86.7 square centimeters.

alt.en: Question 39. Which expression is equivalent to 8x − 2y + x + x? Choices: A: 4x. B: 8x. C: 6x − 2y. D: 10x − 2y.

text.en: “10x −2y ”; This response represents an expression that is equivalent to the given expression. The student may have used a method such as the one below. 8x −2y + x + x = (8x + x + x)−2y = 10x −2y A student who selects this response understands how to apply the properties of operations to generate equivalent expressions.
text.es: “10x −2y ”; This response represents an expression that is equivalent to the given expression. The student may have used a method such as the one below. 8x −2y + x + x = (8x + x + x)−2y = 10x −2y A student who selects this response understands how to apply the properties of operations to generate equivalent expressions.

alt.en: Question 40. Which situation can be represented by the expression 1.3x? Choices: A: the total cost of an item that is x dollars more than $1.30. B: the area of a rectangle with side lengths 1.3 and x. C: the amount of change when $1.30 is used to pay for an item costing x dollars. D: the number of square feet in each lot when 1.3 acres is partitioned into x equal sections.

text.en: : “the area of a rectangle with side lengths 1.3 and x”; This phrase is best represented by the given expression. The student may have observed that the area of a rectangle is the product of the side lengths, which are 1.3 and x, leading to the expression 1.3x. A student who selects this response understands how to represent numbers and write expressions in the context of a real-world problem.
text.es: : “the area of a rectangle with side lengths 1.3 and x”; This phrase is best represented by the given expression. The student may have observed that the area of a rectangle is the product of the side lengths, which are 1.3 and x, leading to the expression 1.3x. A student who selects this response understands how to represent numbers and write expressions in the context of a real-world problem.

alt.en: Question 41. Last year the girls’ basketball team had 8 fifth-grade students and 7 sixth-grade students. What was the ratio of sixth-grade students to fifth-grade students on the team? Choices: A: 8 : 15. B: 8 : 7. C: 7 : 8. D: 15 : 8.

text.en: “7 : 8”; This response represents the correct ratio of sixth-grade students to fifth-grade students on the team. A student who selects this response understands the concept of a ratio and how to use ratio language to describe a ratio relationship between two quantities.
text.es: “7 : 8”; This response represents the correct ratio of sixth-grade students to fifth-grade students on the team. A student who selects this response understands the concept of a ratio and how to use ratio language to describe a ratio relationship between two quantities.

alt.en: Question 42. Peter wants to plot the point (2, 3) on a coordinate plane. Which statement describes how to plot this point starting from the origin? Choices: A: Move 2 units to the left and then 3 units down. B: Move 3 units to the left and then 2 units down. C: Move 2 units to the right and then 3 units up. D: Move 3 units to the right and then 2 units up.

text.en: “Move 2 units to the right and then 3 units up.” This statement describes how to plot the point (2, 3) starting from the origin. The student may have observed that the x-coordinate, 2, would be reached by moving 2 units right, or in the positive direction, and that the y-coordinate, 3, would be reached by moving 3 units up, or in the positive direction. A student who selects this response understands how to use axes and coordinates.
text.es: “Move 2 units to the right and then 3 units up.” This statement describes how to plot the point (2, 3) starting from the origin. The student may have observed that the x-coordinate, 2, would be reached by moving 2 units right, or in the positive direction, and that the y-coordinate, 3, would be reached by moving 3 units up, or in the positive direction. A student who selects this response understands how to use axes and coordinates.

alt.en: Question 44. What is the area, in square centimeters, of a triangle with a horizontal base of 5.8 cm and a perpendicular height of 2.4 cm? The height extends from the top vertex to the base, and a right-angle marker indicates that it is perpendicular to the base. Choices: A: 6.96. B: 10.6. C: 13.92. D: 17.4.

text.en: “6.96” The triangle has a base of 5.8 centimeters and a perpendicular height of 2.4 centimeters. Its area is (1/2) × 5.8 × 2.4 = 6.96 square centimeters.
text.es: “6.96” The triangle has a base of 5.8 centimeters and a perpendicular height of 2.4 centimeters. Its area is (1/2) × 5.8 × 2.4 = 6.96 square centimeters.

alt.en: Question 47. Which expression is modeled by the following diagram? A horizontal number line has arrows at both ends, is labeled 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4, and is divided into fourths between consecutive whole numbers. Beneath it, one bar represents 3 1/4. A second bar of the same total length is partitioned into four consecutive sections labeled 3/4 and one final section labeled 1/4. Choices: A: 3 1/4 ÷ 3/4. B: 3/4 ÷ 3 1/4. C: 3 ÷ 1/4. D: 1/4 ÷ 3.

text.en: “3 1/4 ÷ 3/4” This expression is modeled by the diagram. The model shows 3 1/4 divided into groups of size 3/4, giving four full groups with 1/4 left; the leftover is one third of another 3/4 group, so the quotient is 4 1/3.
text.es: “3 1/4 ÷ 3/4” This expression is modeled by the diagram. The model shows 3 1/4 divided into groups of size 3/4, giving four full groups with 1/4 left; the leftover is one third of another 3/4 group, so the quotient is 4 1/3.

alt.en: Question 48. Points F, G, and H are represented on a coordinate grid. The horizontal axis is labeled x and the vertical axis is labeled y. Both axes begin at 0, and the labeled values 1 through 7 increase by 1. Point F is at (0, 0), point G is at (4, 2), and point H is at (6, 3). Which statement is true for each of the points? Choices: A: The x-coordinate is 1/2 the y-coordinate. B: The x-coordinate is 2 times the y-coordinate. C: The x-coordinate is 2 more than the y-coordinate. D: The x-coordinate is 3 more than the y-coordinate.

text.en: “The x-coordinate is 2 times the y-coordinate.” This statement is true for each of the given points. The student may have compared the x- and y-coordinates for each pair. F(0, 0), G(4, 2), H(6, 3) 2(0) = 0 2(2) = 4 2(3) = 6 A student who selects this response understands how to identify apparent relationships between corresponding terms in a pattern and how to graph ordered pairs consisting of corresponding terms from two patterns.
text.es: “The x-coordinate is 2 times the y-coordinate.” This statement is true for each of the given points. The student may have compared the x- and y-coordinates for each pair. F(0, 0), G(4, 2), H(6, 3) 2(0) = 0 2(2) = 4 2(3) = 6 A student who selects this response understands how to identify apparent relationships between corresponding terms in a pattern and how to graph ordered pairs consisting of corresponding terms from two patterns.

alt.en: Question 49. The coordinates of the vertices of a rectangle are (−2, 3), (4, 3), (4, −4), and (−2, −4). What are the dimensions of the rectangle? Choices: A: 1 unit by 2 units B: 1 unit by 6 units C: 7 units by 2 units D: 7 units by 6 units

text.en: “7 units by 6 units”; This response represents the correct dimensions of the rectangle. The student may have used a method such as the one below. Side 1: (4, 3) and (4, –4), 3−(−4) = 7 Side 2: (4, 3) and (–2, 3), 4 −(−2) = 6 A student who selects this response understands how to find distances between points with the same first coordinate or the same second coordinate.
text.es: “7 units by 6 units”; This response represents the correct dimensions of the rectangle. The student may have used a method such as the one below. Side 1: (4, 3) and (4, –4), 3−(−4) = 7 Side 2: (4, 3) and (–2, 3), 4 −(−2) = 6 A student who selects this response understands how to find distances between points with the same first coordinate or the same second coordinate.

alt.en: Question 51. Lars wrote rules for two patterns. A two-column table has the headings “Pattern for x-values” and “Pattern for y-values.” For the x-values, the starting number is 1 and the rule is: Add 2. For the y-values, the starting number is 1 and the rule is: Multiply by 2. Lars then wrote ordered pairs (x, y) using the patterns above. Which ordered pair could Lars have written? Choices: A: (2, 3) B: (3, 2) C: (3, 6) D: (5, 5)

text.en: “(3, 2)” The x-value pattern is 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, …, while the corresponding y-value pattern is 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, …. Therefore, (3, 2) is an ordered pair produced by the two rules.
text.es: “(3, 2)” The x-value pattern is 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, …, while the corresponding y-value pattern is 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, …. Therefore, (3, 2) is an ordered pair produced by the two rules.

alt.en: Question 52. Noah wants to make a kite out of cloth. The kite is drawn as a four-sided figure with perpendicular dashed diagonals that intersect at a right angle. The horizontal diagonal is divided at the intersection into a left segment of 42.7 cm and a right segment of 22.5 cm. The vertical diagonal is divided at the intersection into an upper segment of 16.3 cm and a lower segment of 16.3 cm. He wants to determine how much cloth he needs. What is the area, rounded to the nearest square centimeter, of Noah’s kite? Choices: A: 531 B: 1,063 C: 1,430 D: 2,126

text.en: “1,063” Decomposing the kite into four right triangles gives 2[(0.5 × 22.5 × 16.3) + (0.5 × 42.7 × 16.3)] = 1,062.76 square centimeters. Rounded to the nearest square centimeter, the area is 1,063.
text.es: “1,063” Decomposing the kite into four right triangles gives 2[(0.5 × 22.5 × 16.3) + (0.5 × 42.7 × 16.3)] = 1,062.76 square centimeters. Rounded to the nearest square centimeter, the area is 1,063.

alt.en: Question 54. Amy, Bart, and Candace each went on a whale-watching trip. On the coordinate plane, x represents the number of hours they spent whale watching and y represents the number of whales seen. The graph is titled “Whales Seen on Trip.” The horizontal axis is labeled “Hours Spent Watching,” and the vertical axis is labeled “Number of Whales.” Both axes begin at 0, with grid lines at each whole number; the labeled values run from 1 through 7. Amy is plotted at (2, 4), Bart is plotted at (3, 4), and Candace is plotted at (2, 2). Which statement is true based on the points plotted on the grid? Choices: A: Bart saw 3 whales in 4 hours. B: Bart saw 1 more whale than Amy. C: Amy and Bart saw the same number of whales. D: Amy and Candace saw the same number of whales.

text.en: “Amy and Bart saw the same number of whales.” This statement in true based on the given points plotted on the grid. The student may have observed that the y-coordinate is the same for the points labeled as “Amy” and “Bart,” and that the y-axis measures the number of whales seen on the trip. A student who selects this response understands how to interpret coordinate values of points in the context of the situation.
text.es: “Amy and Bart saw the same number of whales.” This statement in true based on the given points plotted on the grid. The student may have observed that the y-coordinate is the same for the points labeled as “Amy” and “Bart,” and that the y-axis measures the number of whales seen on the trip. A student who selects this response understands how to interpret coordinate values of points in the context of the situation.

alt.en: Question 55. The base of a right rectangular prism has an area of 173.6 square centimeters and a height of 9 centimeters. What is the volume, in cubic centimeters, of the right rectangular prism? Choices: A: 182.6 B: 781.2 C: 14,061.6 D: 1,562.4

text.en: “1,562.4” The volume of a right rectangular prism is its base area times its height. Therefore, V = Bh = 173.6 × 9 = 1,562.4 cubic centimeters.
text.es: “1,562.4” The volume of a right rectangular prism is its base area times its height. Therefore, V = Bh = 173.6 × 9 = 1,562.4 cubic centimeters.

title.en: New York Grade 7 Math — 2014 Released Questions
title.es: Matemáticas de Nueva York, grado 7 — preguntas publicadas de 2014

description.en: Practice the multiple-choice questions officially released from the 2014 New York State Grade 7 Mathematics Test.
description.es: Practica las preguntas de opción múltiple publicadas oficialmente del examen de Matemáticas de 7.º grado del Estado de Nueva York de 2014.

sourceTitle.en: 2014 NYS Grade 7 Mathematics Test Released Questions
sourceTitle.es: Preguntas publicadas del examen de Matemáticas de 7.º grado del Estado de Nueva York de 2014

alt.en: Question 1. Craig went bowling with $25 to spend. He rented shoes for $5.25 and paid $4.00 for each game. What was the greatest number of games Craig could have played? Choices: A: 4. B: 5. C: 6. D: 7.

text.en: “4” Craig can spend at most $25, so if x is the number of games, 4x + 5.25 ≤ 25. Then 4x ≤ 19.75 and x ≤ 4.9375. Because x must be a whole number, the greatest possible number of games is 4.
text.es: “4” Craig can spend at most $25, so if x is the number of games, 4x + 5.25 ≤ 25. Then 4x ≤ 19.75 and x ≤ 4.9375. Because x must be a whole number, the greatest possible number of games is 4.

alt.en: Question 2. The label on a 1 1/2-pound bag of wildflower seeds states that it will cover an area of 375 square feet. Based on this information, what is the number of square feet that 1 pound of wildflower seeds will cover? Choices: A: 1/250. B: 250. C: 562 1/2. D: 750.

text.en: “250” A 1 1/2-pound bag covers 375 square feet. Dividing by 1 1/2 gives the coverage for one pound: 375 ÷ (3/2) = 375 × (2/3) = 250 square feet.
text.es: “250” A 1 1/2-pound bag covers 375 square feet. Dividing by 1 1/2 gives the coverage for one pound: 375 ÷ (3/2) = 375 × (2/3) = 250 square feet.

alt.en: Question 3. Which expression is equivalent to (7x − 5) − (3x − 2)? Choices: A: 10x − 7. B: 10x − 3. C: 4x − 7. D: 4x − 3.

text.en: ““4x – 3”; This response represents the correct equivalent expression. (7x −5)−(3x −2) = 7x −3x −5−(−2) = 4x −3
text.es: ““4x – 3”; This response represents the correct equivalent expression. (7x −5)−(3x −2) = 7x −3x −5−(−2) = 4x −3

alt.en: Question 4. The Lions won 16 games last year. This year the Lions won 20 games. What is the percent increase in the number of games the Lions won from last year to this year? Choices: A: 20%. B: 25%. C: 80%. D: 125%.

text.en: “25%” The team won 20 − 16 = 4 more games. Relative to last year's 16 wins, the percent increase is (4/16) × 100% = 25%.
text.es: “25%” The team won 20 − 16 = 4 more games. Relative to last year's 16 wins, the percent increase is (4/16) × 100% = 25%.

alt.en: Question 5. The table shows prices for shoe rental, games, and snacks at the bowling alley. The table is titled “BOWLING ALLEY PRICES” and has columns labeled “Item” and “Price.” The rows are: Shoe rental, $2.75; One game of bowling, $2.50; Small soda, $0.95; Large soda, $1.50; Nachos, $1.75. Gina rented shoes, bowled 3 games, and bought 1 order of nachos. She used a coupon for 1/2 off the price of her bowling games. What was Gina’s total cost before tax was added? Choices: A: $5.75. B: $6.00. C: $8.25. D: $12.00.
